_meta.topic: Define Use Cases and Quality Standards for Existing Data
_meta.description: Data profiling, data quality rules, cleansing requirements, and use case validation for existing data assets.

mcq.q: Why should a solution architect define quality standards for existing data early in a PL-600 project?
options: To make reports look more polished
To determine whether existing data is fit for the intended business use cases and what remediation effort is required
To avoid discussing migration
To ensure all historical data is copied unchanged
mcq.explanation: Data quality standards should be tied to how the data will actually be used. The architect needs to know whether existing data is accurate, complete, consistent, and usable enough for the target business scenarios, and what cleansing or transformation effort will be needed.
wrongExplanations: Visual polish is not the main reason to define data quality standards.
Data quality work usually increases the need for migration planning rather than avoiding it.
Copying low-quality data unchanged often transfers legacy problems into the new solution.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: Which question best helps validate whether existing data is suitable for a proposed use case?
options: Does the source system use a modern color palette?
Does the data contain the required values, quality level, and history needed to support the target process or decision?
How many users like the current export format?
Was the legacy database built by an internal team?
mcq.explanation: The architect should test data against the specific business use case. The key issue is whether the data has the right fields, completeness, timeliness, and quality for the intended future-state scenario.
wrongExplanations: Visual design does not determine whether data supports a business use case.
Export preference does not validate data suitability.
Who built the database does not determine whether the data is fit for purpose.

msRef.label: Manage your data effectively with the right architecture

mcq.q: A stakeholder says, "We already have customer data, so migration should be easy." What should the architect do first?
options: Accept the assumption and reduce migration planning
Profile the data and validate completeness, consistency, duplicates, and required transformations against the target use cases
Ignore the existing data until UAT
Assume Dataverse will fix the data automatically
mcq.explanation: Having data is not the same as having usable data. The architect should validate actual quality characteristics and transformation needs before accepting migration assumptions.
wrongExplanations: This is a risky assumption because existing data often contains issues that materially affect effort and scope.
Data quality issues should be assessed early, not deferred to UAT.
Dataverse does not automatically correct poor source data.

msRef.label: Using the data profiling tools - Power Query

mcq.q: Which statement best describes data profiling in this context?
options: A branding exercise for dashboards
An activity to understand the structure, completeness, distribution, and anomalies in existing data
A way to bypass requirements workshops
A replacement for business process analysis
mcq.explanation: Data profiling helps the architect understand the shape and condition of the data, including completeness, distinct values, patterns, and anomalies that affect solution design and migration effort.
wrongExplanations: Branding has nothing to do with data profiling.
Data profiling complements, not replaces, discovery and requirements work.
Process analysis is still necessary because good data alone does not define the target process.

msRef.label: Using the data profiling tools - Power Query

mcq.q: Why should data quality rules be linked to business use cases rather than defined as generic cleanup goals?
options: Because cleanup should focus only on aesthetic formatting
Because the required level of quality depends on how the data will be used in the future solution
Because generic cleanup eliminates the need for profiling
Because all data must meet the same quality threshold
mcq.explanation: Not every data element needs the same standard. Quality requirements should be driven by the business scenario, such as reporting, automation, matching, or operational decision-making.
wrongExplanations: Formatting may matter, but it is not the main reason to define quality standards.
Profiling is still required to understand current data condition.
Different use cases often require different quality thresholds and controls.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: Which is the best example of a useful data quality rule for existing customer records?
options: Customer records should look cleaner
Each active customer record must have a unique identifier, a valid primary contact method, and no unresolved duplicate match above the agreed threshold
Users should like the imported data
Addresses should be modern
mcq.explanation: A good quality rule is specific and testable. This example defines concrete conditions tied to usability and data trustworthiness.
wrongExplanations: This is too vague to validate or operationalize.
User sentiment is not a sufficient data quality rule.
Modern is subjective and not measurable.

msRef.label: Data Cleansing - Data Quality Services (DQS)

mcq.q: What is the strongest architect reason to identify cleansing requirements during solution assessment rather than during final cutover preparation?
options: Because cleansing never affects scope
Because cleansing effort can materially affect timeline, ownership, migration design, and feasibility
Because cleansing is only a DBA concern
Because cutover teams prefer fewer meetings
mcq.explanation: Cleansing work can be large, time-consuming, and dependent on business ownership. It affects scope, planning, migration sequencing, and whether phase-one goals are realistic.
wrongExplanations: Cleansing often affects scope significantly.
Cleansing is not only a DBA issue; business and data owners usually need to participate.
Meeting preference is irrelevant to why cleansing should be identified early.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: A team wants to use legacy product data for automated recommendations in the new system. Which concern should the architect validate first?
options: Whether the recommendation screen looks modern
Whether the product data is complete, consistent, and trustworthy enough for the automation use case
Whether the product table has enough columns
Whether the old system used a different font
mcq.explanation: When data will drive automation or decisions, the architect must validate that it is sufficiently complete and reliable for that specific use case.
wrongExplanations: Screen appearance does not determine whether data can support automation.
Column count alone does not show whether the data is good enough.
Font differences are irrelevant.

msRef.label: Manage your data effectively with the right architecture

mcq.q: Which finding most strongly indicates that existing data quality may block a future-state process?
options: The source application has an old user interface
Critical fields required for routing and automation are missing or inconsistent across records
A report takes too long to open
The data owner prefers spreadsheets
mcq.explanation: If the future-state process depends on key fields that are missing or inconsistent, the data quality issue is directly blocking business capability and must be handled early.
wrongExplanations: Old UI does not prove process-blocking data issues.
Report speed is not the main issue described.
Tool preference does not by itself prove that required data is unusable.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: What is the best way to judge whether historical data should be cleansed before migration or left behind?
options: Migrate everything because storage is cheap
Assess whether the historical data supports required business, reporting, audit, or operational use cases in the target solution
Move only the newest records regardless of business need
Let developers decide without stakeholder input
mcq.explanation: Historical data decisions should be based on actual business value and required use cases, not blanket rules. Some data may need cleansing and migration, some may need archiving, and some may not need to move at all.
wrongExplanations: Cheap storage does not justify migrating low-value or poor-quality data unnecessarily.
Recency alone is not a sound decision rule.
Stakeholders and data owners are needed because this is a business decision as much as a technical one.

msRef.label: Manage your data effectively with the right architecture

tf.q: True or False: Data profiling can help expose completeness, distribution, and anomaly issues in existing data before migration or reuse.
tf.explanation: Microsoft's profiling guidance shows how profiling helps reveal data quality, distribution, and column-level issues that matter for downstream use and migration.

msRef.label: Using the data profiling tools - Power Query

tf.q: True or False: A data quality rule should ideally be measurable and tied to whether the data can support a defined business use case.
tf.explanation: Quality standards are most useful when they are explicit and connected to the business outcome the data must support.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

tf.q: True or False: If source data exists, the architect can assume it is suitable for automation and analytics use cases without validation.
tf.explanation: Data existence does not prove data fitness. The architect should validate quality and suitability against the actual use case before relying on it.

msRef.label: Manage your data effectively with the right architecture

tf.q: True or False: Cleansing requirements can affect scope and timeline, so they should be identified during solution assessment.
tf.explanation: Cleansing is often a significant workstream and can materially affect delivery planning and feasibility.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

dragdrop.instruction: Match each data concern to the most relevant assessment activity.
items: Many customer records are blank in key contact fields
The same product appears under multiple inconsistent names
The business wants to automate routing based on priority values
No one knows whether old warranty history is still needed
zones: Use case validation against quality rules

correctMapping.The business wants to automate routing b: Use case validation against quality rules

dragdrop.explanation: These concerns map to different architect activities: understanding completeness, resolving inconsistency, checking whether data can support a future use case, and deciding whether historical data has business value.

msRef.label: Using the data profiling tools - Power Query

dragdrop.instruction: Match each architect action to the reason it matters.
items: Validate data against future-state use cases
zones: Turns vague quality expectations into testable standards
Makes remediation planning realistic and accountable
Confirms whether data is fit for the intended business purpose

correctMapping.Define explicit quality rules: Turns vague quality expectations into testable standards
correctMapping.Identify cleansing ownership: Makes remediation planning realistic and accountable
correctMapping.Validate data against future-state use c: Confirms whether data is fit for the intended business purpose

dragdrop.explanation: A strong architect approach connects each data-quality activity to a concrete decision need instead of treating data cleanup as a generic technical task.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

casestudy.title: Case Study - Existing Data Looks Fine Until the Use Case Changes
casestudy.scenario: A company plans to move case management to Dynamics 365. Stakeholders say the existing customer and warranty data is already available in legacy systems, so they expect migration to be straightforward. During discovery, the business also wants automated case routing based on region and product line, proactive reporting on repeat issues, and agent dashboards showing complete contact details. Initial profiling reveals many blank contact fields, inconsistent product naming, duplicate customer records, and warranty history with missing region values. Business leaders still argue that the data is 'good enough' because users have been working with it for years.

msRef.label: Study guide for Exam PL-600: Microsoft Power Platform Solution Architect

questions.q: What should the architect do first?
options: Accept that the data is good enough because the business has used it historically
Validate the existing data against the specific future-state use cases and define quality standards accordingly
Defer all data analysis until cutover planning
Assume the routing rules can be simplified to avoid data issues
questions.explanation: The right first move is to test the data against the intended future-state use cases. Data that worked in the past may not be sufficient for automation, analytics, or standardized routing.
wrongExplanations: Historic usability does not prove future fitness.
That is too late because data quality may shape scope and feasibility.
Simplification may help, but the architect should first understand the actual gap between data quality and use-case needs.

msRef.label: Manage your data effectively with the right architecture

questions.q: Which data issue most directly threatens automated case routing?
options: Users prefer spreadsheets
Missing region values and inconsistent product naming
The legacy system is old
A dashboard opens slowly
questions.explanation: Automated routing depends on reliable routing attributes. Missing region values and inconsistent product names directly undermine that capability.
wrongExplanations: Tool preference is not the direct blocker to routing logic.
System age alone does not prove routing failure.
Dashboard speed is not the key issue for routing.

msRef.label: Manage your data in Dynamics 365 implementation projects

questions.q: Why is the statement 'users have been working with it for years' a weak defense of current data quality?
options: Because past manual workarounds do not prove the data is fit for standardized automation and reporting
Because old data should always be deleted
Because Dynamics 365 does not support historical data
Because profiling is unnecessary if users are experienced
questions.explanation: Data that users can work around manually may still be too incomplete or inconsistent for automated routing, analytics, or trustworthy dashboards.
wrongExplanations: Historical data can still be valuable if it supports valid use cases.
Dynamics 365 can support historical data; the issue is data quality and fit.
User experience does not remove the need for profiling.

msRef.label: Using the data profiling tools - Power Query

questions.q: What is the best next planning action after confirming these quality issues?
options: Ignore them because the migration budget is fixed
Define cleansing requirements, ownership, and scope based on the future-state data use cases
Move all bad records into a hidden table
Abandon the project immediately
questions.explanation: Once the quality gap is known, the architect should turn it into a concrete workstream with explicit cleansing requirements, ownership, and scope decisions tied to business value.
wrongExplanations: Budget pressure does not eliminate data reality.
Hiding bad data is not the same as fixing or managing it.
The right response is structured mitigation, not immediate abandonment.

msRef.label: Data Cleansing - Data Quality Services (DQS)
